Author: Sai Methukupally
Title: Enhancing Agentic AI Web Applications with Human-in-the-Loop AI Using LangGraph
Text:
Enhancing Agentic AI Web Applications with Human-in-the-Loop AI Using LangGraph
Sai Methukupally (https://www.linkedin.com/in/sai-pranav-reddy/)
Human-in-the-loop (HITL) design is critical when AI systems make decisions that must be reviewed for accuracy, compliance, or safety. While frameworks like LangGraph make agent orchestration easy, adding HITL checkpoints inside a web app requires handling concurrency, state persistence, and exact workflow resumption. This article shows how to implement that end-to-end pattern with practical, production-ready techniques.
Agentic Workflows with HITL
In this blog, we’ll walkthrough how to add a human‑in‑the‑loop checkpoint to a LangGraph workflow running inside a full-stack web app with separate frontend and backend containers. You’ll see how to pause an agent mid flow, send an interrupt payload to the browser, store a checkpoint tied to a session, accept an approval or correction, and resume at the exact point without losing state or duplicating work.
Need
· Human checkpoints before critical agent actions
· 100% accurate entity/date (or other key) extraction
· Safeguarding sensitive, regulated, or ambiguous decisions
· Reliable human oversight in a multi-user web app
Problem
· Agent must pause mid workflow and later resume exactly
· Harder in concurrent, multithreaded frontend/backend environment
· Must persist and identify each conversation session
· Frontend must tell normal output from an interrupt request
· Multiple simultaneous sessions increase coordination complexity
Gap
· Public HITL examples focus on simple terminal flows
· Lack of session-aware checkpoint + deterministic resume pattern
· No guidance on distinguishing interrupt payloads in the UI
· Missing patterns for multi-attempt corrections under concurrency
Solution
· LangGraph state graph with typed shared state
· Interrupt node returning structured payload for the frontend
· Persisted checkpoints keyed by session / HITL IDs
· Resume command to rehydrate state and continue exactly at prior node
· Frontend logic to detect interrupt vs normal response and collect approval/correction
High Level Functional flow
1. User query enters via the web app chat UI.
2. The agentic workflow starts by determining the intent behind the user’s input and routes the conversation accordingly.
3. Key entities are extracted from the user’s query. The challenge was that our dictionary contained thousands of very similar entities. We needed the AI system to pick the correct one, or the downstream analysis would be wrong. To guarantee that, we added an HITL step so the user can validate the extracted entity before the workflow continues.
4. The workflow pauses for user approval or correction.
5. Once the information is approved or corrected, the workflow resumes analysis and generates the final payload.
6. The final payload is delivered back to the user in the web app chat UI.
8. When the user responds, the system must determine whether this is a response to the interrupt or a new question.
- If it’s a new question, the solution will ignore the prior conversation and start fresh
- If it’s a confirmation/adjustment regarding the entities that were identified, the workflow will continue.
9. After the user interrupt is handled, the identified entities are 100% validated so the workflow completes and generates the full response based on the selected entities.
Detailed Technical flow
Many of the concepts such as agent state, interrupts and unique LangGraph concepts will be explained in further detail further down this blog.
1. The user submits a query in the web chat.
2. The backend starts (or continues) a LangGraph StateGraph run using a thread_id that maps to the session and initializes the AgentState.
3. An intent_classifier node reads the current AgentState, infers intent, writes intent fields, and a conditional edge routes the flow based on those values.
4. An entity_extraction node pulls a candidate entity and date from a large dictionary and stores them in AgentState.
5. Because an incorrect entity or date would corrupt the later analysis, the state is marked to require human validation before any analytical nodes run.
6. A human_approval node sees that no prior approval exists and returns an interrupt Command with a structured payload.
7. LangGraph checkpoints the AgentState in the persistence layer keyed by the thread (and any HITL identifier).
8. Execution pauses.
9. The frontend receives the interrupt payload, displays the proposed entity and date, and the user replies with either approval or a correction.
10. The backend receives this reply, locates the saved checkpoint by the same thread_id (and HITL identifier), and confirms it addresses the pending interrupt.
11. The backend constructs a resume Command containing approved status and any correction, invokes the graph, and LangGraph rehydrates the AgentState from the checkpoint and continues past the interrupt boundary.
12. Analysis nodes now run with validated data, appending tables, summaries, and any derived metrics to AgentState; further conditional edges may branch but no longer need human intervention for this cycle.
13. The workflow reaches END and returns the final AgentState (or a pruned result object) to the frontend as a normal response, completing this validated pass.
Why we chose LangGraph for HITL Orchestration
We chose LangGraph because it natively supports three things we needed for human checkpoints: interrupts (to stop mid node boundary), checkpointing (to persist per session), and resume commands (to continue exactly where we paused). Together they let us pause at a critical step, collect one or more rounds of user feedback, and then continue without rebuilding context.
· Interrupt: A node can return an interrupt payload; execution halts and state is checkpointed.
· Checkpoint: Persisted snapshot keyed by session/thread id (and optional HITL id).
· Resume Command: Instruction carrying user approval or correction; reloads checkpoint and continues.
State Graph & Agent State
A LangGraph StateGraph is a workflow engine for orchestrating complex, multi-step processes using nodes and edges. Each node represents a function (step) that processes a shared state. The graph controls the flow of execution by connecting nodes with edges, including conditional branches based on the state.
Key concepts:
· StateGraph: The workflow container; nodes + edges form the directed flow.
· Agent State: you can think of the state as a dictionary that stores all the useful information generated by each node, think of it as the run time memory of the graph and it’s usually a dictionary or TypedDict
· Nodes: Functions that process or transform the state.
· Edges: Connections between nodes, determining the order of execution.
· Conditional Edges: Branching logic that routes the workflow based on state values.
· Entry Point: The starting node of the workflow.
# --- Compose the graph ---
workflow = StateGraph(AgentState)
# 1. Add all nodes
workflow.add_node("context_and_history", context_and_history_node)
workflow.add_node("intent_classifier", intent_classifier_node)
workflow.add_node("respond_direct", respond_direct_node)
workflow.add_node("entity_date", entity_date_node)
workflow.add_node("human_approval", human_approval_node)
workflow.add_node("route_after_approval", route_after_approval)
workflow.add_node("variance_analysis_node", variance_analysis_node)
workflow.add_node("end", end_node)
# 2. Set entry point
workflow.set_entry_point("context_and_history")
workflow.add_edge("context_and_history", "intent_classifier")
# 3. Branch after intent detection
workflow.add_conditional_edges(
"intent_classifier",
intent_branch,
{
"entity_date": "entity_date",
"respond_direct": "respond_direct",
}
)
# 4. Variance pipeline: entity_date → human_approval → Variance Analysis → end
# workflow.add_edge("entity_date", "human_approval")
workflow.add_conditional_edges(
"entity_date",
entity_date_branch,
{
"human_approval": "human_approval",
"end": "end"
}
)
# 5. Use conditional routing after human_approval
workflow.add_conditional_edges(
"human_approval",
route_after_approval,
{
"variance_analysis_node": "variance_analysis_node",
"to_end_node": "end"
}
)
# 6. Variance Analysis: variance_analysis_node → end
workflow.add_edge("variance_analysis_node", "end")
# 7. Non-variance: respond_direct → end
workflow.add_edge("respond_direct", "end")
# 8. Set finish point
workflow.set_finish_point("end")
Defining a node
You then define each of these nodes, for example:
workflow.add_node("context_and_history", context_and_history_node)
This is the first node in our application and it’s responsible for fetching any historical context.
As you can see, this node is called with the state dict and at the very end, after all the information has been processed,
it adds the information to the state to be passed on to the next node.
async def context_and_history_node(state: dict) -> dict:
payload = state.get("payload")
print("[debug]Starting context_and_history_node with payload:", state)
# 1. Validate payload
try:
if isinstance(payload, dict):
payload = InputPayload(**payload)
except ValidationError as ve:
print(f"Invalid input payload: {ve}")
return {**state, "error": f"Invalid input payload: {ve}"}
# 2. Build DB connection and history
try:
if not all([DB_USER_NAME, DB_PASSWORD, DB_URL]):
raise ValueError("Database credentials or URL are missing.")
user_enc = quote_plus(DB_USER_NAME)
password_enc = quote_plus(DB_PASSWORD)
match = re.match(r"jdbc:postgresql://([^:/]+):(\d+)/(.*)", DB_URL)
if not match:
raise ValueError("Invalid JDBC URL format")
host, port, dbname = match.groups()
connection_string = f"postgresql://{user_enc}:{password_enc}@{host}:{port}/{dbname}"
try:
history = PostgresChatHistory(session_id=payload.session_id, connection_string=connection_string)
except Exception as e:
print("Connection to PostgresSQL failed:", e)
return {**state, "error": f"Postgres connection failed: {e}"}
except Exception as e:
print("Error setting up PostgreSQL connection:", e)
return {**state, "error": f"DB setup failed: {e}"}
# 3. Fetch previous context
try:
previous_context = history.get_context(payload.session_id, payload.user_id)
except Exception as e:
print("Error fetching previous context:", e)
previous_context = []
# Optionally: return or raise, depending on your app's requirements
# 4. Return enriched state
history = None
return {
**state,
"payload": payload,
"history": history,
"previous_context": previous_context,
}
How to setup conditional edges
I’ll explain the use of conditional edges using this example
workflow.add_edge("context_and_history", "intent_classifier")
# 3. Branch after intent detection
workflow.add_conditional_edges(
"intent_classifier",
intent_branch,
{
"entity_date": "entity_date",
"respond_direct": "respond_direct",
}
)
Here, we create a function called intent classifier which uses LLM to identify the topic of conversation. Based on that, it will fill in the agent state. Once we have the values in agent state, we can use the conditional edges to route the conversation in multiple different ways based on the output of the intent classifier node.
try:
llm_json = json.loads(response.message.content.strip('`').replace('json', ''))
if llm_json.get("status") == "Success":
# Mark error in state; downstream nodes can handle or route to end
return {
**state,
"is_variance_question": True,
"llm_message": llm_json.get("message", "")
}
else:
# LLM ran, but NOT a variance question (or domain-specific error)
return {
**state,
"is_variance_question": False,
"llm_message": llm_json.get("message", "")
}
This node will direct to entity date node if it’s a variance question, otherwise, will prompt the LLM to respond directly.
# routes the conversation based on the intent
def intent_branch(state: AgentState) -> str:
print("[DEBUG] intent branch node called with:", state) # Add for debugging
return "entity_date" if state.get("is_variance_question") else "respond_direct"
This function is called in your conditional edge:
# 3. Branch after intent detection
workflow.add_conditional_edges(
"intent_classifier",
intent_branch,
{
"entity_date": "entity_date",
"respond_direct": "respond_direct",
}
)
The intent_branch
function acts as a router, examining the agent state and returning a string that matches one of the keys in the conditional edge mapping. If the returned value doesn't match any key, LangGraph will raise an error or you can define a default fallback node.
Conditional edges make your workflow highly extensible. For example, you can add more branches for other types of questions by updating the mapping and the branch function logic.
Always test your branch functions with various state scenarios to ensure correct routing.
Conditional edges make your workflow highly extensible. You can:
- Add new branches by updating the mapping and branch function logic.
- Support more complex decision trees by chaining conditional edges.
- Easily modify routing logic without changing the overall workflow structure.
Agent State
Within the StateGraph, there is a shared state class that is shared by the nodes in your workflow. In our example, this class is called agent state.
Each state populates this class, and upon reaching an end node, the workflow concludes. The LangChain orchestrator then returns the final output, which includes all key information gathered from each node in the process.
In the example below, you can see the various required and optional variables that would get populated as it goes through the workflow.
# --- Define state ---
class AgentState(TypedDict):
# Core context
payload: Any
history: Optional[PostgresChatHistory]
previous_context: list
# Intent classification
llm_message: Optional[str]
is_variance_question: Optional[bool]
# Direct response
response: Optional[str]
# Entity/date extraction and HITL
extracted_entity_date: Optional[dict]
extracted_entities: Optional[list]
date_filter: Optional[dict]
response_status: Optional[int]
rebuilt_prompt: Optional[str]
is_new_question: Optional[bool]
# Human approval (HITL)
approved: Optional[bool]
attempt_count: Optional[int]
last_correction: Optional[str]
correction_processing_error: Optional[bool]
# Variance analysis
analysis_table: Optional[dict]
analysis_report: Optional[dict]
# Final outputs and error handling
final_api_response: Optional[dict]
api_response: Optional[dict]
error: Optional[str]
The workflow is modular and extensible; each step is defined as an async function. Because we’re using a state graph, each module operates independently and can be added, removed, or tested in isolation, which makes the system especially robust and modular.
Interrupt, Checkpointing and Resume Commands
Now that the concepts of StateGraph and Agent State are clear, we can investigate the concepts of interrupt, checkpointing and resume which are the key tools used to enable HITL.
Checkpointing: At any point where human input is needed (e.g., approval or correction), the workflow is interrupted, and the current state is saved as a checkpoint in a persistent store (like SQLite).
Interrupt: The workflow returns an interrupt payload, pausing execution and waiting for human feedback.
Resume Command: When the human provides feedback, a resume command is issued with the new data (e.g., approved or correction). The workflow loads the checkpointed state and continues from where it left off, using the human input to proceed.
Key Challenge
The key challenge here is when dealing with a full stack application, you need to fully terminate the process at the interrupt stage and re start the process from the point where it was interrupted. When doing this in terminal, it’s quite simple when you don’t have a
The workflow is checkpoint-ed for resume-ability, using session identifiers to resume interrupted flows. To understand checkpointing, you need to understand the concept of a
Interrupt example
Here is the human approval node and there are some key features that were incorporated here:
1. The user has 3 tries to make corrections if the solution did not pick the correct
2. You define the interrupt payload
3. You then return this payload using the syntax below and when this point is reached, the workflow comes to a halt and this payload is sent to the front end.
#updated human approval node to allow up to 3 correction rounds
async def human_approval_node(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
"""
Shows extracted entity/date to user, allows up to 3 correction rounds.
On each correction, rewrites and re-extracts before proceeding.
"""
max_attempts = 3
payload = state["payload"]
prev_context = state.get("previous_context", [])
original_query = payload.user_query
attempt_count = state.get("attempt_count", 0)
print("[DEBUG] human_approval_node called with state:", state)
# Check if we've exceeded attempts from a previous correction loop
if attempt_count >= max_attempts:
print(f"[DEBUG] Max attempts ({max_attempts}) reached. Failing.")
return {
**state,
"approved": False,
"error": "Max correction attempts exceeded.",
}
# Always start with current extraction
attempt_count = state.get("attempt_count", 0)
last_good_entity = state.get("extracted_entity_date", {}).get("extracted_entity")
last_good_date = state.get("extracted_entity_date", {}).get("date_period")
last_response_status = state.get("response_status")
extracted = state.get("extracted_entity_date", {})
rebuilt_prompt = state.get("rebuilt_prompt", original_query)
entities = extracted.get("extracted_entity")
date_filter = extracted.get("date_period")
#inturupt payload that matches the final output structure in END NODE
interrupt_payload = JsonResponse(
status="Interrupted",
message=MessageBlock(
response=(
"Are the extracted date and entity/entities correct? If yes, reply with 'Yes'. "
"If not, please provide a correction (e.g., 'No, the date should be Jan 2021').\n"
f"Entities: {entities}\n"
f"Date Filter: {date_filter}"
),
entity_date_filter={
"entities": entities,
"date_filter": date_filter
},
query=None,
chain_of_thought=None,
overall_variance=None,
analysis_tables=None,
detail_summary=None,
overall_summary=None
)
).model_dump()
return interrupt(interrupt_payload)
Checkpointing example
At any point where human input is needed (e.g., approval or correction), the workflow is interrupted, and the current state is saved as a checkpoint in a persistent store (like SQLite).
Here’s how we’re using it here:
1. As soon as the workflow starts, this is the first function that’s called.
2. This function checks if this is a net new conversation or if it’s conversation that’s been checkpointed
3. If it’s net new, it creates a new agent state and initializes the flow, if it’s a net new conversation, it’s going to check for a correction and continue the flow from where it was left off using.
4. The only real nuance here on top of traditional checkpointing is the ability to also send corrections because from a functional standpoint, we’re asking the user if the information extracted from our search index (e.g. Azure AI Search, Elastic, or OpenSearch) is correct, if they say yes, great, if not, they can say something like ‘No, it’s actually ___” and we would take that correction and incorporate it into the flow.
Resume Command: The resume command is what tells the agent to resume from a certain node:
1. You first create your resume data that you want to pass when resuming your workflow.
resume_data = {
"approved": is_approval,
"correction": None if is_approval else user_text
}
2. Once you’re resume data is created, you then create the command
resume_command = Command(resume=resume_data)
3. Once the command is created, you can pass it to initialize the workflow and continue the flow from where it last stopped.
result2 = await app.ainvoke(resume_command, config=config)
# Function to run or resume the HITL demo workflow
async def run_or_resume_hitl_demo(payload_obj, follow_up_payload_obj, workflow, db_path):
async with AsyncSqliteSaver.from_conn_string(db_path) as checkpointer:
app = workflow.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)
config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": payload_obj.HITL_session_id}}
# 1. Try to get the checkpoint to see if we need to resume
checkpoint = await checkpointer.aget(config)
is_interrupted = checkpoint and "__interrupt__" in checkpoint.get("versions_seen", {})
print(f"Is interrupted: {is_interrupted}")
if not is_interrupted:
# Initial state setup
initial_state = {
"payload": payload_obj,
"history": None,
"previous_context": [],
"attempt_count": 0
}
result = await app.ainvoke(initial_state, config=config)
print("--- Initial workflow result ---")
print(f"Status: {'interrupted' if '__interrupt__' in result else 'completed'}")
checkpoint1 = await checkpointer.aget(config)
# For demo purposes we’re printing the checkpoint. In production, avoid logging user content or sensitive session data
print("Checkpoint after first payload:")
print(json.dumps(checkpoint1, indent=2, default=str))
if result.get("__interrupt__"):
# Prepare for resume
print("\n=== Step 2: User Submits Approval ===")
user_text = follow_up_payload_obj.user_query
is_approval = await is_approval_by_llm(user_text)
resume_data = {
"approved": is_approval,
"correction": None if is_approval else user_text
}
print(f"Resuming with: {resume_data}")
resume_command = Command(resume=resume_data)
result2 = await app.ainvoke(resume_command, config=config)
print("--- Final workflow result ---")
if result2.get("error"):
print(f"Error: {result2['error']}")
else:
print(f"Response: {result2.get('response', 'No response available')}")
else:
print("Workflow completed without interruption")
else:
# Already interrupted, resume directly
print("\n=== Resuming interrupted workflow ===")
user_text = follow_up_payload_obj.user_query #change to normal payload
#user_text = payload_obj.user_query
is_approval = await is_approval_by_llm(user_text)
resume_data = {
"approved": is_approval,
"correction": None if is_approval else user_text
}
print(f"Resuming with: {resume_data}")
resume_command = Command(resume=resume_data)
result2 = await app.ainvoke(resume_command, config=config)
print("--- Final workflow result ---")
if result2.get("error"):
print(f"Error: {result2['error']}")
else:
print(f"Response: {result2.get('response', 'No response available')}")
print("result2:")
print(result2)
We can now explore how the front end integrates with the backend leveraging a HITL framework
1. Frontend: Detecting Interrupt Payloads and Prompting User
- The backend sends a response to the frontend.
- If the workflow is interrupted (HITL required), the response contains a special interrupt payload.
- The frontend must detect this and prompt the user for approval or correction.
Example Backend Response (Interrupt Payload)
{
"status": "Interrupted",
"message": {
"response": "Are the extracted date and entity/entities correct? If yes, reply with 'Yes'. If not, please provide a correction.",
"entity_date_filter": {
"entities": [...],
"date_filter": {...}
},
"query": null,
"chain_of_thought": null,
"overall_variance": null,
"analysis_tables": null,
"detail_summary": null,
"overall_summary": null
}
}
Frontend Detection Example (React/JS)
// Assume 'apiResponse' is the response from backend
if (apiResponse.status === "Interrupted") {
// Show approval/correction UI
showApprovalPrompt(apiResponse.message.response, apiResponse.message.entity_date_filter);
} else {
// Show normal result
showResult(apiResponse.message);
}
Prompting the User
- Display the extracted entities and date filter.
- Ask for approval (“Yes”) or correction (text input)
2. Frontend: Sending Resume Command to Backend
When the user responds, the frontend sends a follow-up payload to the backend to resume the workflow.
Resume Payload Example
{
"session_id": "SESSION_ID_HERE",
"user_id": "USER_ID_HERE",
"HITL_session_id": "HITL_SESSION_ID_HERE",
"user_query": "Yes", // or correction text
"filters": { ... }
}
3. Backend: Processing Resume Command
API Contract
- The backend expects the same payload structure as the initial query, but
user_query
contains the approval or correction. - The backend uses the session and HITL_session_id to resume the correct workflow.
Backend Resume Logic
async def HITL_LangGraph_Orch(payload_obj, workflow, db_path):
# ...existing code...
if is_interrupted:
# Treat this payload as the correction/confirmation
user_text = payload_obj.user_query
is_approval = await is_approval_by_llm(user_text)
resume_data = {
"approved": is_approval,
"correction": None if is_approval else user_text
}
resume_command = Command(resume=resume_data)
result = await app.ainvoke(resume_command, config=config)
# ...existing code...
- Frontend: prompts user for approval/correction, and sends a resume payload.
- Backend: Receives the resume payload, uses session info to resume the workflow, and processes the user’s input (approval or correction).
- This pattern ensures seamless HITL interaction between frontend and backend, allowing workflows to pause and resume with human input.
Session and State Management in HITL Workflows
Why IDs Matter
- user_id: Uniquely identifies the user interacting with the system.
- session_id: Tracks a specific conversation or workflow instance for a user.
- HITL_session_id: Used to manage and resume human-in-the-loop interruptions, especially in multi-threaded or multi-user environments.
How IDs Are Used
- Every request from the frontend includes these IDs in the payload.
- The backend uses them to:
- Retrieve previous context and chat history for the user/session.
- Save checkpoints and workflow state so the conversation can be paused and resumed.
- Ensure that when a user responds to an interrupt, the correct workflow instance is resumed (not someone else’s or a different session).
Persistence and Retrieval
- AsyncSqliteSaver (or Postgres):
- Stores the current state of the workflow, keyed by session/HITL_session_id.
- On new requests, checks if a checkpoint exists for the session.
- If interrupted, resumes from the saved state using the IDs.
- If not, starts a new workflow.
Why This Is Important
- Guarantees that each user’s conversation is isolated and can be resumed exactly where it left off.
- Prevents mix-ups between users or sessions, which is critical for compliance, accuracy, and user experience in HITL scenarios.
Summary
To enable HITL in a web app chatbot, use a stateful orchestrator like LangGraph to manage workflow interruptions and resumptions. Human agents are engaged only when needed, allowing automation to continue seamlessly after human input. This approach supports robust, modular, and extensible agentic AI workflows.
Great reads to help for technical Development
https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/concepts/human_in_the_loop/
https://shaveen12.medium.com/langgraph-human-in-the-loop-hitl-deployment-with-fastapi-be4a9efcd8c0
